doctrinal_notes: In 経 as 'sutra,' the character renders Sanskrit sūtra — itself a word meaning 'thread' or 'cord,' applied to canonical texts conceived as threading doctrinal points together in a continuous narrative. In 経行 (kinhin, walking meditation), the verbal sense is doing the work: 経 names the passing-through, the sustained traversal that walking meditation enacts. The semantic continuity between the noun 'sutra' and the verb 'pass through' is not accidental — both involve a continuous thread that holds together what would otherwise be discrete moments.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the first wave of Buddhist transmission as the standard rendering of Sanskrit sūtra. The compound 仏経 (the Buddha's sutras) and the names of specific sutras (心経, the Heart Sutra; 法華経, the Lotus Sutra) integrate the character into Japanese Buddhist vocabulary at the foundational level. The verbal sense survives in non-Buddhist compounds and in the technical Zen term 経行.

gloss: Walking, going, conduct, practice — a crossroads with figures in motion; the action of going, extended to all directed activity including the path of Buddhist practice.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A pictograph of a crossroads — two intersecting paths viewed from above
graphic_evolution.notes: The original image is concrete: a crossroads where paths meet, the place of going and coming. From this the meanings 'to go, to walk, to travel' all develop, and from the verb of going the meanings 'conduct, action, practice' extend. By the classical period 行 covers the full range from physical walking to ritual practice to moral conduct.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 6 strokes — preserves the crossroads structure with two parallel verticals connected by horizontals
graphic_evolution.notes: In Japanese 行 has multiple readings reflecting its semantic range: こう (going, traveling), ぎょう (action, practice, line), あん (special compounds like 行脚 angya, religious pilgrimage). It appears in countless compounds: 行く (iku, to go), 行動 (kōdō, action), 行為 (kōi, deed), 修行 (shugyō, practice), 行持 (gyōji, sustained practice), 経行 (kinhin, walking meditation).

doctrinal_notes: 行 is one of the most polysemous and load-bearing characters in Buddhist vocabulary. As 'practice' it covers the entire field of Buddhist activity — meditation, study, ethical conduct, ritual. As 'going' it provides the verbal core of compounds like 経行 (walking meditation) and 行脚 (pilgrimage). In Yogācāra psychology 行 (xíng / saṃskāra) names one of the five aggregates (skandha), referring to mental formations or volitional impulses. Across all these uses the underlying logic is consistent: 行 names directed activity, the going-toward that constitutes both physical walking and the sustained practice of the Buddhist path.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present in Japanese writing from the Nara period in the full range of its meanings. Its load-bearing Buddhist registers — practice, conduct, mental formations — develop with the Nara-period scholastic transmission and are preserved across all Japanese Buddhist schools. The compound 修行 (shugyō, religious practice) is one of the most commonly used Buddhist terms in modern Japanese.

gloss: Only, just, simply — a restrictive particle marking what is the case and nothing more; in 只管 the marker of unmixed singularity, doing one thing without admixture.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 口 (mouth) with two diverging strokes below — possibly a particle marker spoken from the mouth
graphic_evolution.notes: The semantic core is restrictive: 'only this and not other.' From this the meanings 'just, merely, simply, alone' all develop. In modern usage 只 functions as the literary 'only, just' (ただ in kun reading).

graphic_evolution.form_description: 5 strokes — 口 (3 strokes) above two diverging strokes
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 只 is used in literary and formal contexts. Its load-bearing Zen use is in the compound 只管 (shikan, single-mindedly, purely), which forms the first half of 只管打坐 (shikantaza, just sitting) — Dōgen's name for objectless meditation.

doctrinal_notes: In 只管 (shikan) and 只管打坐 (shikantaza, just sitting), 只 is the restrictive particle that does the doctrinal work of refusing any addition to the bare activity of sitting. 'Just' sit — not sit-and-something-else, not sit-in-order-to-achieve, not sit-while-thinking-about, but just sit. The 只 is small but doctrinally crucial: it is the character that prevents zazen from being mixed with any other goal or technique. Sōtō Zen's identity as the school of objectless meditation is built on this restrictive force.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present in Japanese writing from the Nara period in literary and formal usage. Its load-bearing Zen use in 只管打坐 belongs specifically to Dōgen's Sōtō tradition, in which the compound becomes the school's signature term for the practice of objectless sitting.

gloss: A pipe, a tube; to manage, to be in charge — extended through grammatical use as a particle of single-minded focus; in 只管 the second character that intensifies 'only' to 'wholly, exclusively'.

components.meaning: bamboo radical (6 strokes) — semantic element: the original referent is a bamboo pipe
official (8 strokes) — phonetic and partly semantic element: a pipe that has charge of something, a conduit that manages flow

graphic_evolution.form_description: 竹 (bamboo) over 官 (official) — a bamboo conduit
graphic_evolution.notes: The original meaning is concrete: a bamboo pipe, used for conducting water, sound, or other things in a single channel. From this the meaning extends to: tube, pipe, channel, and figuratively to managing or being in charge of something — exercising control over a single domain.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 竹 (6 strokes) over 官 (8 strokes) — 14 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In ordinary Japanese 管 means 'pipe, tube' (as in 管楽器, kangakki, wind instruments) and 'to manage' (管理, kanri, management). Its function in 只管 is grammatical: paired with 只, it intensifies the restrictive force from 'only' to 'exclusively, wholly.' The compound 只管 functions adverbially in classical Chinese as 'single-mindedly, purely, exclusively.'

doctrinal_notes: In 只管打坐 (shikantaza, just sitting), 管 combines with 只 to form the adverbial compound 只管 (shikan), which intensifies the restrictive force of 只 alone. The semantic logic of pairing 'only' with 'pipe/channel' is consistent: a single channel admits only one flow at a time, single-mindedly conducting one thing through to its destination. 只管打坐 is the practice of sitting through a single channel — the activity of sitting is admitted whole and unmixed, with no admixture of goal or technique.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present in Japan from the Nara period in its concrete and managerial senses. Its grammatical use as the second half of 只管 belongs to classical Chinese adverbial usage and is preserved in Japanese primarily through the Sōtō Zen vocabulary of 只管打坐.

gloss: To strike, to do, to carry out — a hand performing an action; in 打坐 the verb that turns 'sit' into 'do sitting,' marking the act as something performed rather than merely undergone.

components.meaning: hand radical (3 strokes) — semantic element: an action of the hand
nail, the fourth heavenly stem (2 strokes) — phonetic element

graphic_evolution.form_description: 扌 (hand) beside 丁 (phonetic) — the hand striking
graphic_evolution.notes: The primary meaning is 'to strike, to hit' — a concrete physical action of the hand. By extension 打 functions in classical Chinese as a generic verb of doing, performing, or carrying out an action: the hand engaged in any task. This generalized usage makes 打 productive as a verbal prefix in compounds.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 扌 (3 strokes) beside 丁 (2 strokes) — 5 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 打 is widely productive as both 'strike' (打撃, dageki, blow) and as a generic verbal prefix (打開, dakai, breakthrough). In 打坐 (taza), 打 turns the noun 坐 (sitting) into a verbal phrase: doing-sitting, performing-sitting, the act of sitting carried out as an action.

doctrinal_notes: In 只管打坐 (shikantaza, just sitting), 打 is doing precise grammatical work: it turns 坐 (sit) into 打坐 (do sitting, perform sitting), naming the act as something actively performed rather than passively assumed. The compound 只管打坐 thus reads more precisely as 'just-doing-sitting' — single-mindedly engaging in the activity of sitting, with no admixture of any other goal or technique. The 打 prevents the practice from being read as mere physical posture: zazen is something one does, an activity carried out, not a position one occupies.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present in Japanese writing from the Nara period in its general meaning of 'striking, hitting, doing.' Its grammatical use in 打坐 belongs to Chan/Zen technical vocabulary and is preserved in Japanese primarily through the Sōtō term 只管打坐.

gloss: To sit — two persons facing each other on the ground; the act of sitting that the practice of zazen takes as its entire physical form.

components.meaning: earth radical (3 strokes) — the ground on which the sitting takes place
person (2 strokes), repeated as two figures facing each other above the earth

graphic_evolution.form_description: Two figures facing each other above the earth element — persons sitting on the ground in mutual presence
graphic_evolution.notes: The original image is concrete and relational: two persons sitting facing each other on the earth. The semantic core is the act of sitting as a sustained presence, not merely the postural position. From this concrete sense the meaning extends to all forms of sitting, including the meditative.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 7 strokes — two 人 elements above 土
graphic_evolution.notes: Distinct from 座 (also pronounced ざ in Japanese), which adds a covering element and means 'seat' as a noun. 坐 is the verb of the act of sitting; 座 is the place or position where one sits. In Buddhist contexts 坐禅 uses the verbal 坐 to name the act of sitting in meditation, while 座席 (seki/seat) and 高座 (kōza/high seat) use the nominal 座.

doctrinal_notes: In 坐禅 (zazen, seated meditation), 坐 names the entire physical activity of the practice. Sōtō Zen takes this with extreme literalness: the activity of zazen is precisely the activity of sitting — not what the mind does while the body sits still, but what the whole embodied person does in the act of sitting. Dōgen's Fukanzazengi (Universal Recommendation of Zazen) treats the postural details of 坐 — the position of the legs, the alignment of the spine, the position of the hands, the orientation of the eyes — as integral to the practice rather than preparatory to it. The character 坐 carries this full weight: zazen is the activity of sitting, all the way down.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present in Japan from the Nara period in its general meaning of 'sitting.' Its load-bearing Zen use in 坐禅 arrives with the Kamakura transmission of Chan: Eisai (Rinzai) and Dōgen (Sōtō) both establish 坐禅 as the central practice of their respective lineages, and 坐 in this technical sense remains the primary vocabulary of Japanese Zen practice instruction to the present day.

gloss: Meditation, contemplation, dhyāna — the focused absorption of meditation, transliterated and reabsorbed into Chinese as the name of the school built around it.

components.meaning: altar/spirit radical (4 strokes) — semantic element marking the religious register
single, simple (9 strokes in simplified form) — phonetic element providing the chán sound

graphic_evolution.form_description: Traditional 禪: 示 (altar) beside 單 (phonetic) — assembled to render the Sanskrit dhyāna
graphic_evolution.notes: The character was assembled to phonetically transliterate the Sanskrit dhyāna (or Pali jhāna) — the focused absorption of meditation as analyzed in Buddhist psychology. The 示 radical signals the religious-meditative register; 單 (and its simplified form 单) provides the chán sound. The traditional form 禪 is still used in Taiwan and Hong Kong; mainland China and Japan use the simplified 禅.

graphic_evolution.form_description: Simplified to 禅 (13 strokes): 示 beside the simplified 単
graphic_evolution.notes: The character functions both as the noun for meditation (the activity) and as the name of the school built around the practice (Chan in Chinese, Zen in Japanese, Sŏn in Korean, Thiền in Vietnamese). The fact that the school takes its name from the practice rather than from any doctrinal content is itself doctrinally significant.

doctrinal_notes: 禅 carries two distinct but inseparable senses in East Asian Buddhist vocabulary. As a common noun, it names the meditative absorption (dhyāna) that all Buddhist traditions practice in some form. As a proper noun, it names the school of Chinese Buddhism — and its descendants in Korea, Japan, and Vietnam — that takes meditation as the central activity through which awakening is occasioned. The school's name is 禪宗 (the meditation school), and its self-understanding emphasizes that what makes it distinctive is not a body of doctrine but a particular relationship to the practice of meditation. The Chan/Zen claim is that doctrine, sutra study, and ritual practice are all subordinate to direct meditative engagement with the nature of mind, and the character 禪 encodes this priority.
japanese_transmission: Arrives in Japan as part of the general Buddhist meditative vocabulary in the Nara and Heian periods, but becomes a distinct school name only with Eisai's introduction of Rinzai Zen (1191) and Dōgen's introduction of Sōtō Zen (1227). From the Kamakura period onward, 禅 in Japanese refers primarily to the Chan/Zen school as a distinct institutional and practice tradition, while the more general meditative sense survives in compounds like 禅定 (zenjō, meditative absorption).

gloss: Level, even, ordinary — a flat balance scale; in Chan/Zen vocabulary the qualifier of the unexceptional everyday in which the Way is directly available.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A flat horizontal element with vertical balance — possibly a balance scale or a level surface
graphic_evolution.notes: The semantic core is consistent: 平 names what is level, balanced, even. From this it extends to 'peaceful' (the leveling of conflict), 'flat' (geometric levelness), and 'ordinary' (what is unexceptional, on the same level as everything else). The Chan/Zen use of 平 in 平常 (ordinary, everyday) is the third sense — what is on the level of everything else, unexceptional.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 5 strokes — a balanced symmetric form preserving the leveling sense
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 平 is widely productive: 平和 (heiwa, peace), 平等 (byōdō, equality), 平凡 (heibon, ordinary), 平成 (Heisei, the era name 1989–2019), and the Chan compound 平常心 (byōjōshin, ordinary mind).

doctrinal_notes: In 平常心是道 (ordinary mind is the Way), 平 contributes the sense of what is on the level — unexceptional, not elevated above the everyday. The doctrinal point of the formula is precisely that the Way is not found in extraordinary states or special practices but in the ordinary functioning of the mind right now. 平 is doing the leveling work: the Way is on the same level as everyday experience, not above it.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present in Japan from the Nara period across legal, administrative, and Buddhist registers. Its load-bearing Chan/Zen use in 平常心 arrives with the Kamakura transmission and is preserved in the canonical formula 平常心是道.

gloss: Constant, ordinary, always — what holds steady through change; in Chan/Zen the qualifier of the unchanging everyday from which the Way is never absent.

graphic_evolution.form_description: An upper element over 巾 (cloth radical) — possibly a long garment that hangs constantly
graphic_evolution.notes: The semantic core: what is constant, what does not change, what is always there. From this the meanings 'usual, ordinary, eternal, the norm' all develop. In Buddhist contexts 常 takes on a contested doctrinal weight: the Three Dharma Seals declare that all conditioned things are 無常 (impermanent), so 常 is structurally a problematic predicate — yet it appears positively in compounds like 常住 (eternally abiding) and 涅槃常住 (nirvāṇa eternally abiding) in Tathāgatagarbha-influenced traditions.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 11 strokes — an upper element over 巾
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 常 carries the senses 'usual, ordinary, always, constant': 常識 (jōshiki, common sense), 日常 (nichijō, everyday), 常時 (jōji, always), 平常心 (byōjōshin, ordinary mind). The Buddhist sense of 'eternally abiding' is technical and confined to doctrinal contexts.

doctrinal_notes: In 平常心 (ordinary mind) and 平常心是道 (ordinary mind is the Way), 常 contributes the sense of what is always already there — the constancy of the everyday mode of mind through all moments of experience. Combined with 平 (level, unexceptional), 平常 names what is unexceptionally present and constantly available. The Chan/Zen use is doctrinally sharp: the Way is not what arises during special practice or extraordinary states but the constant ordinary functioning of mind across all moments.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present from Nara times across multiple registers. Its doctrinal use in Mahāyāna Buddhism is contested: the Three Dharma Seals make 無常 (impermanence) a foundational claim, while Tathāgatagarbha and certain Mahāyāna sutras affirm 常 as a positive predicate of Buddha-nature. The Chan/Zen use in 平常心 sidesteps the debate by using 常 in the everyday sense rather than the metaphysical sense.

gloss: Immediately, just-this — a kneeling figure beside a vessel of food, the moment of approaching to eat; in Buddhist usage the marker of immediate identity rather than mediated relation.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A kneeling person beside a raised food vessel — the moment of approaching to eat
graphic_evolution.notes: The original image is concrete: a person kneeling at a meal, just about to eat. From this concrete sense the abstract meaning of 'immediately, just at this moment, right here' develops. Classical Chinese commentators emphasized that 即 names not merely temporal immediacy but a relationship of identity — 'just this and not other.'

graphic_evolution.form_description: An upper element (vessel) over 卩 (kneeling figure radical) — 7 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 即 functions as the literary 'namely, that is to say' (すなわ.ち) and as the prefix marking immediate succession or identity: 即時 (sokuji, immediately), 即位 (sokui, accession), 即心是仏 (sokushin zebutsu, this very mind is Buddha).

doctrinal_notes: In Buddhist usage 即 carries the heaviest doctrinal weight in compounds asserting non-dual identity. 色即是空 (form is precisely emptiness) — the Heart Sutra's pivotal formula — uses 即 to assert that form and emptiness are not two related things but identically one. 即心是仏 (this very mind is Buddha) — the Mazu Daoyi formulation that became a Chan/Zen slogan — uses 即 to collapse the distance between the practitioner's actual functioning mind and Buddhahood. The 即 in these formulas is doing precise work: it refuses any mediating relation. Not 'mind is connected to Buddha' or 'mind leads to Buddha,' but mind is, just-this, Buddha. The character is the workhorse of non-dualist assertion in East Asian Buddhist vocabulary.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present from the Nara period and used in the standard Buddhist non-dualist formulas from the earliest stratum of Japanese Buddhist literature. Its load-bearing register belongs to Mahāyāna doctrinal vocabulary throughout, with the Chan/Zen slogans of the Kamakura period (即心是仏) reactivating its potency for the practical pedagogy of the Zen tradition.

gloss: Is, this, correct — the verb of identification and the demonstrative pointing at what is the case; in Buddhist non-dual formulas the copula that does the load-bearing identifying work.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 日 (sun) over 正 (correct, upright) — the sun standing straight, that which is correctly so
graphic_evolution.notes: The classical etymology connects 是 to the sun at its highest point, the moment of unmistakable position. From this the meanings 'correct, this very thing, is' develop. In classical Chinese 是 is the standard demonstrative ('this') and one of the standard copulas ('is').

graphic_evolution.form_description: 日 (sun, 4 strokes) over 疋 (variant of 正, 5 strokes) — 9 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 是 is rare outside of fixed phrases and Buddhist or classical contexts. It survives in 是非 (zehi, 'right and wrong' or 'by all means') and in the Buddhist non-dual formulas: 色即是空 (shiki soku ze kū, form is precisely emptiness), 即心是仏 (sokushin zebutsu, this very mind is Buddha).

doctrinal_notes: In the cardinal Buddhist non-dual formulas, 是 is the copula doing the identification. 色即是空 — form precisely is emptiness. 即心是仏 — this mind is Buddha. The character's function in these formulas is grammatical but doctrinally heavy: 是 is what makes the assertion an identity rather than a comparison or association. Read carefully, the 是 in these formulas refuses any reading that would treat the two terms it joins as separate entities being related. It says they are, just-this, the same.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with first-wave Buddhist transmission as part of the technical vocabulary for translating Mahāyāna sutras. The Heart Sutra's 色即是空 — the most chanted line in East Asian Buddhism — keeps 是 in active liturgical use across all Japanese Buddhist schools to the present day, even though the character is otherwise rare in modern Japanese.

gloss: Body, person — originally a pictograph of a pregnant woman in profile; the lived body as the site of practice and the locus of awakening, not its obstacle.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A pregnant woman in profile, the swollen abdomen clearly visible
graphic_evolution.notes: The original meaning is 'pregnant body' — the woman whose body has become the site of new life. From this concrete sense the meaning extends to: the body generally, oneself, one's person. The pictographic origin is among the more vivid in the writing system.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 7 strokes — a vertical structure preserving the curved shape of the original
graphic_evolution.notes: In ordinary Japanese 身 covers the body, oneself, a person, and one's social position. In Chan/Zen vocabulary it appears in the load-bearing compound 身心 (body-mind) and in 身心脱落 (casting off body-mind), Dōgen's term for his pivotal awakening.

doctrinal_notes: In 身心一如 (shinjin ichinyo, body-mind as one) and 身心脱落 (shinjin datsuraku, casting off body-mind), 身 is paired with 心 (heart-mind) to name the unified field of embodied experience. The doctrinal point is to refuse a dualism between body and mind: practice happens in and as the embodied person, not in a disembodied consciousness that uses the body as its instrument. Dōgen's emphasis on zazen as the activity of the whole body-mind, not merely a mental discipline conducted while the body sits still, is built on this refusal.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, mapped onto the native Japanese word み (mi, body, oneself) from the earliest stratum of Japanese literacy. Its doctrinal weight in 身心 compounds belongs primarily to the Kamakura Zen transmission, in which Dōgen's writings make the body-mind unity a foundational principle.

gloss: One, single, unity — the most elemental stroke; in Buddhist usage the qualifier of unity, identity, and the single condition that holds across apparent multiplicity.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A single horizontal stroke
graphic_evolution.notes: The simplest character in the writing system: a single horizontal line. The semantic content is direct — the number one, oneness, unity. From this primitive sense the character extends to: the same, identical, single, undivided.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 1 stroke — a single horizontal line, unchanged from the oracle bone form
graphic_evolution.notes: Functionally one of the most common characters in Japanese, appearing in countless compounds: 一人 (hitori, one person), 一日 (ichinichi, one day), 一切 (issai, all), 一如 (ichinyo, oneness-suchness), 統一 (tōitsu, unification), 第一 (daiichi, first).

doctrinal_notes: In Buddhist compounds 一 carries the load of unity and non-duality. 一如 (ichinyo, oneness-suchness) names the single thusness that holds across apparent multiplicity. 一切 (issai, all, the totality) extends 'one' to mean 'one and the same throughout.' 身心一如 (body-mind as one) refuses the body-mind dualism by asserting their identity. The character is small but doctrinally heavy — every claim about non-duality in East Asian Buddhism is built on the work 一 does in compounds.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, the most common numeral and one of the most frequent characters overall in Japanese writing. Its doctrinal use in Buddhist compounds is integral to the vocabulary from the Nara period onward.

gloss: Self, oneself, from — originally a pictograph of the nose; in Chinese philosophical and Buddhist usage the root of self-reference and the marker of what arises spontaneously from a thing itself.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A stylized depiction of a nose viewed from the front
graphic_evolution.notes: The original meaning is 'nose,' and the original use of 自 to mean 'self' may derive from the gesture of pointing at one's nose to indicate oneself — a gesture still common in modern Chinese and Japanese culture. The semantic shift from 'nose' to 'self' is one of the more vivid in classical Chinese.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 6 strokes — a rectangular form with horizontal interior strokes, fully abstract from the original pictograph
graphic_evolution.notes: The 'nose' meaning has been entirely displaced by the 'self / from / by oneself' meanings. 自 is productive in compounds: 自分 (jibun, oneself), 自然 (shizen, nature, what is so of itself), 自性 (jishō, self-nature), 自由 (jiyū, freedom, 'from oneself'), 自我 (jiga, ego).

doctrinal_notes: In 自性 (jishō, self-nature), 自 carries the Sanskrit svabhāva that Madhyamaka Buddhism most insistently denies — the inherent, independent self-existence that things would need in order to exist apart from their causes. Nāgārjuna's argument is that nothing has 自性, because everything arises in dependence on conditions. In Tathāgatagarbha and Chan/Zen contexts the term is sometimes used positively — 'see one's self-nature, become Buddha' — creating a doctrinal tension that careful commentators have to manage. The character is also central to 自然 (shizen, what is so of itself, nature) and to the Chan/Zen vocabulary of spontaneity (自由, 自在 — freedom, self-mastery).
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present in Japanese writing from the Nara period and immediately mapped onto the native Japanese reflexive みずから (mizukara, by oneself) and the adverbial おのずから (onozukara, of itself, naturally). The Buddhist register of 自性 arrives with the Nara-period scholastic transmission. Its modern productivity is enormous — 自 is one of the most common characters in Japanese and appears in countless compounds across all registers.

gloss: Dharma — water flowing in its proper course; the Buddha's teaching, the order of phenomena, and the law of how things are, all carried in a single character.

components.meaning: water radical (3 strokes) — semantic element: a process flowing in its course, regulated by its nature
to go (5 strokes) — semantic and partially phonetic element: the going-out, the departure, what proceeds

graphic_evolution.form_description: 氵 (water) beside an earlier complex element including 廌 (a mythical horned creature of justice) — water and the creature that judges
graphic_evolution.notes: The classical etymology connects 法 to a mythical tradition in which a horned beast was used in trials of justice — water (氵) representing the leveling principle, the beast representing the discernment of right from wrong. The character was simplified early in its history to drop the beast component, leaving the modern form. The semantic core: a regulated, principled order — what flows in its proper course, the way things go.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 氵 (3 strokes) on the left beside 去 (5 strokes) — 8 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In ordinary classical Chinese 法 means 'law, statute, method, principle.' Buddhist translators recruited it to render Sanskrit dharma — the Buddha's teaching, the constitutive elements of phenomena, and the law of how things are — exploiting the polysemy of the original. In modern Japanese 法 covers law (法律), method (方法), and dharma (仏法), with the religious register marked by context.

doctrinal_notes: 法 is the East Asian rendering of Sanskrit dharma, one of the most polysemous and load-bearing terms in Buddhist vocabulary. Dharma covers: (1) the Buddha's teaching as a body of doctrine, (2) the elementary constituents of phenomenal experience as analyzed in Abhidharma psychology, (3) the law or principle of how things are, and (4) phenomenon or thing in the most general sense. 法 carries all four registers, with context determining which is foreground. In compounds: 仏法 (the Buddha's teaching), 諸法 (all phenomena), 法性 (the nature of dharmas), 法界 (the dharma-realm), 法身 (the dharma-body of a Buddha). Chan/Zen exploits the polysemy: 法 can be the formal teaching to be transmitted and simultaneously the bare reality of things prior to any teaching about them.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the first wave of Buddhist transmission in the Nara period as the standard rendering of Sanskrit dharma. The native Japanese word のり (nori, law, principle) is mapped onto it as the kun reading. The character is among the most frequent in Japanese Buddhist vocabulary and appears in countless compounds across all schools. The non-Buddhist sense of 'law, method' also enters Japanese early and remains the dominant register in legal and technical contexts (法律 hōritsu, 方法 hōhō).

gloss: True, real, genuine — the unfeigned condition of a thing as it is, prior to ornamentation or distortion; in Buddhist usage the qualifier of reality as it is before conceptual elaboration.

graphic_evolution.form_description: A complex form combining elements possibly related to a vessel and observation
graphic_evolution.notes: The original etymology is disputed; Han-period commentators connected it to the image of an immortal or Daoist sage in their unaltered nature. The semantic core that survives is unambiguous: 真 names what is real and unfeigned, contrasted with 偽 (false, fabricated).

graphic_evolution.form_description: 10 strokes — a vertical structure with 目 (eye) embedded in the upper portion
graphic_evolution.notes: Used across Chinese and Japanese for 'true, real, genuine.' Productive in compounds: 真理 (shinri, truth), 真実 (shinjitsu, fact, truth), 真如 (shinnyo, suchness), 真心 (magokoro, sincere heart). The Buddhist register in 真如 is technical.

doctrinal_notes: In 真如 (shinnyo), 真 names reality as it is in itself, prior to and beyond conceptual elaboration. The character pairs with 如 (thusness, just-as-it-is) to render Sanskrit tathatā — the suchness of phenomena, their nature when not overlaid by the discriminating activity of the mind. 真 in this compound is doing limiting work: it specifies that the 'thusness' in question is not appearance but the actual reality, the true thusness as opposed to a mistaken or partial one.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present in Japanese writing from the Nara period and immediately mapped onto the native Japanese word まこと (sincerity, truth). Its Buddhist register develops with the Nara-period Yogācāra and Madhyamaka transmissions, in which 真如 (Sanskrit tathatā) becomes a load-bearing technical term. In modern Japanese 真 is one of the most common characters and carries a wide range of senses from doctrinal to colloquial.

gloss: Thusness, just-as — to be like, to correspond, to be in accordance; in Buddhist usage the suchness of phenomena, their condition just as they are without conceptual overlay.

components.meaning: woman radical (3 strokes) — semantic element with disputed origin in this character
mouth (3 strokes) — semantic element: a mouth speaking what corresponds, what is in accordance

graphic_evolution.form_description: 女 (woman) beside 口 (mouth) — semantic logic disputed
graphic_evolution.notes: Classical commentators offered various accounts: a woman speaking in accordance, or 女 as phonetic with 口 as semantic. The semantic core that survives is the sense of 'being like, being in accordance, just as.' From this concrete sense the abstract meaning of 'thusness' (the way a thing is) develops.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 女 (3 strokes) beside 口 (3 strokes) — 6 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In ordinary Japanese 如 functions as the literary 'just as, like' (ごと.し). Its load-bearing Buddhist use is in 真如 (shinnyo, suchness) and 如来 (nyorai, Tathāgata, 'thus-come' — the standard epithet of a Buddha). Both terms render Sanskrit tathā and its derivatives — the 'thus' that names reality as it is.

doctrinal_notes: In 真如 (shinnyo, suchness) and 如来 (nyorai, Tathāgata, 'thus-come one'), 如 renders Sanskrit tathā — the 'thus' that names things as they are without conceptual addition. The doctrinal point of 如 is its refusal to add anything: reality as it is requires no characterization beyond 'just thus.' The Tathāgata is one who has 'thus come' and 'thus gone' — one whose movement is in accordance with the suchness of things rather than against it. The compound 真如 doubles the emphasis: the true thusness, reality just as it is and not otherwise.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the first wave of Buddhist scriptural transmission in the Nara period as part of the technical vocabulary for translating Sanskrit tathatā and tathāgata. The compounds 真如 and 如来 are both fully integrated into Japanese Buddhist usage from the earliest period and remain stable across all Japanese Buddhist schools. The kun reading ごと.し survives in literary and classical Japanese as the conjunction 'just as, like,' but the doctrinal register belongs to the Buddhist stratum.

gloss: To open — two hands lifting the bar from a closed gate; in 開悟 the verb of inception, naming the moment awakening first dawns as the opening of what was closed.

components.meaning: gate (8 strokes) — the semantic frame: doors of a closed gate, between which the action takes place
two hands lifting (4 strokes in simplified form) — the semantic element: hands raising the bar that holds the gate shut

graphic_evolution.form_description: 門 (gate) with an interior element depicting two hands and a bar — the act of removing the bar
graphic_evolution.notes: The original image is concrete: hands lifting the bar from a closed gate. From this concrete sense the meaning extends to: opening generally, beginning, inaugurating, making accessible. The figural use in 開悟 (opening to awakening) preserves the structural sense — what was closed becomes accessible.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 門 (8 strokes) framing an interior element — 12 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 開 is widely productive: 開く (hiraku, to open), 開始 (kaishi, commencement), 開発 (kaihatsu, development), 開悟 (kaigo, opening to awakening). The doctrinal register in 開悟 is technical and confined to Buddhist usage.

doctrinal_notes: In 開悟 (kaigo, opening to awakening), 開 is the verb of inception. The character does not name the completeness of awakening — that is 大悟 — but the moment of opening, the breakthrough through which awakening first becomes accessible. The figural logic is consistent with 開 elsewhere: what was closed off becomes available, the gate is unbarred, the path is opened. In Chan/Zen contexts 開悟 tends to be used for the initial breakthrough moment rather than for the full and final realization, though the line between 開悟 and the more emphatic 大悟 is not always sharply drawn.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present in Japan from the earliest stratum of literacy and immediately mapped onto the native Japanese verbs ひらく (hiraku) and あく (aku). Its doctrinal use in 開悟 arrives with the Chan transmission in the Kamakura period and remains a stable but not dominant term in the Zen vocabulary of awakening — less prominent than 悟り or 大悟 but a recognized formulation. In ordinary modern Japanese 開 is one of the most common characters and carries no Buddhist resonance outside of fixed compounds.

gloss: To become, to complete — a halberd marking the moment a thing reaches its full form; in 成仏 the verb of attainment, becoming what one already structurally is.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 戈 (halberd) with a small mark — possibly indicating completion or settlement
graphic_evolution.notes: The original sense is disputed. Some commentators read the halberd as marking the moment of victory or settlement of an affair; others see it as a phonetic component. The semantic core that survives into classical and modern usage is unambiguous: 成 names the moment a thing reaches its completed form.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 6 strokes — a complex form with 戈 (halberd) on the right side
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese, 成 is widely productive: 成る (naru, to become), 完成 (kansei, completion), 成功 (seikō, success), 成仏 (jōbutsu, becoming Buddha). Across these uses the underlying logic is consistent — a process reaching its fulfilled form.

doctrinal_notes: In 成仏 (jōbutsu, becoming Buddha) and 見性成仏 (kenshō jōbutsu, seeing one's nature, becoming Buddha), 成 is the verb of attainment. The doctrinal subtlety is that the becoming is not the production of something new but the manifestation of what was already structurally the case. Chan/Zen exploits this tension: 成仏 is what practice aims at and simultaneously what one already is — the becoming is not a transition between states but a recognition that requires no transition.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, mapped onto the native Japanese verb なる (naru, to become) from the earliest stratum of Japanese literacy. Its load-bearing Buddhist use in 成仏 arrives with the Nara-period Buddhist transmission and remains stable across all Japanese Buddhist schools — every tradition uses 成仏 as the standard verb for attaining Buddhahood. In modern colloquial Japanese 成仏 also functions as a euphemism for dying peacefully, with the implication that the deceased has reached liberation.

gloss: Buddha — a person (亻) marked as transcendent or extraordinary; phonetic transliteration of Sanskrit buddha, the awakened one, foundational to all Buddhist vocabulary in East Asia.

components.meaning: person radical (vertical form of 人, 2 strokes) — the semantic element: a Buddha is a kind of person, an awakened being
private, self (2 strokes) — phonetic element in the simplified form

graphic_evolution.form_description: Traditional 佛: 亻 (person) beside 弗 (phonetic, providing the fó sound) — assembled to render Sanskrit buddha
graphic_evolution.notes: The traditional form 佛 was coined specifically to transliterate Sanskrit buddha. The 亻 radical signals that the referent is a kind of person; 弗 provides the phonetic value. The character has no native Chinese semantic content prior to Buddhist usage — it was assembled from existing components to fit the doctrinal vocabulary.

graphic_evolution.form_description: Simplified to 仏 (4 strokes): 亻 (person) beside 厶 (a simple residual element)
graphic_evolution.notes: The Japanese postwar simplification (新字体, shinjitai) replaces 弗 with 厶, reducing the stroke count substantially. The simplified form is now universal in Japanese print. Mainland Chinese simplified script uses 佛 → 佛 (no change in PRC simplification, though traditional 佛 remains in Taiwan and Hong Kong).

doctrinal_notes: The single most important character in Buddhist vocabulary. 仏 is the Buddha — Śākyamuni in his historical reference, the awakened ones generally as a class, and the Buddha-nature in beings as a doctrinal claim. The character appears in countless compounds: 仏教 (Buddhism), 仏性 (Buddha-nature), 仏心 (Buddha-mind), 成仏 (becoming Buddha), 念仏 (recitation of the Buddha's name). Chan/Zen's distinctive contribution is the assertion that the Buddha is not elsewhere — not a deity to be supplicated, not a historical figure to be venerated from a distance, but the very functioning of one's own awakened nature: 即心是仏 (this very mind is Buddha).
japanese_transmission: Arrives in Japan with the formal introduction of Buddhism in the 6th century CE — traditionally dated to 538 or 552 with the Korean Baekje court's gift of Buddhist images and texts to the Yamato court. The character is among the earliest Buddhist loans into Japanese and has been continuously in use since. The kun reading ほとけ (hotoke) is a native Japanese word with disputed etymology — possibly from 'hotoborkeshi' (one who has cooled passion) or from a Korean loan — that became the standard kun reading. In modern Japanese 仏 covers the full range of Buddhist meanings and also functions as a respectful term for the dead (ほとけさま, hotokesama), reflecting the cultural identification of death with attaining Buddhahood through 成仏.

gloss: Death — a person bowing beside the residue of bones; in Chan/Zen the figural ego-death (大死) that must precede genuine awakening, the dissolution of the seer-seen structure.

components.meaning: bone fragment, residue of death (4 strokes) — semantic element naming what is left after life departs
person bowed or kneeling (2 strokes) — semantic element: a figure in mourning posture

graphic_evolution.form_description: A bone fragment beside a kneeling figure — a person mourning beside the remains of the dead
graphic_evolution.notes: The original image is concrete and ritual: a living person in attendance on the residue of one who has died. The semantic logic carries: 死 names not the abstract event of dying but the relationship between the living and what death has left behind.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 歹 (bone fragment, 4 strokes) on the left beside 匕 (kneeling figure, 2 strokes) — 6 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: The pictographic logic remains visible. 死 functions in everyday Japanese as the standard verb for dying (死ぬ shinu). Its doctrinal weight in Chan/Zen is concentrated in compounds like 大死 (daishi, the great death) and 生死 (shōji, birth-and-death) — in each case the term is used not to name biological death but the structural transformations of consciousness that the figure of death allows the tradition to point at.

doctrinal_notes: In Chan/Zen, 死 is used in two doctrinally important compounds. In 生死 (shōji), it pairs with 生 (birth) to name saṃsāra — the cycle of birth-and-death from which awakening liberates, but which Dōgen's Shōji fascicle insists is the very locus of liberation. In 大死 (daishi, the great death), 死 names the figural ego-death that must precede genuine awakening: the complete dissolution of the ordinary subject-object structure of consciousness, the death of the self that grasps at experience as its possession. The Linji school in particular emphasizes 大死 as the necessary condition for 大悟 — only one who has died the great death can be born into the great life that awakening reveals.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present from the earliest stratum of Japanese literacy and immediately mapped onto the native Japanese verb しぬ (shinu, to die). Its doctrinal use in Chan/Zen compounds — 大死, 生死, 死活 — arrives with the Kamakura transmission and remains technical, confined to Buddhist contexts. In ordinary modern Japanese 死 is the workaday term for death and dying.

gloss: Beginning, inception — the moment a process initiates; in 始覚 the qualifier marking awakening as something arrived at by a particular practitioner at a particular time, contrasted with 本覚's structural priority.

components.meaning: woman radical (3 strokes) — semantic element with associations to origin and birth
platform, foundation (5 strokes) — phonetic element providing the shǐ sound

graphic_evolution.form_description: 女 (woman) beside 台 (phonetic) — semantic and phonetic elements
graphic_evolution.notes: Classical commentators glossed 始 as the moment a thing first comes into being — explicitly tied through the female radical to images of birth and origin. The semantic logic is straightforward: 始 names the first instant of any process, the inception point from which a thing extends.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 女 (3 strokes) on the left beside 台 (5 strokes) — 8 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern usage 始 is the workhorse character for 'begin, start': 始める (hajimeru, to start), 開始 (kaishi, commencement), 始末 (shimatsu, beginning and end of an affair). Its doctrinal weight in 始覚 is technical and almost entirely confined to Tendai and Zen contexts.

doctrinal_notes: In 始覚 (shikaku, acquired or initiated enlightenment), 始 marks the temporal arrival of awakening for a particular practitioner — the moment recognition first dawns, in contrast to 本覚 (hongaku) which marks awakening as the structural prior. The pair are not opposed positions but two moments of a single doctrine: 本覚 names what is realized; 始覚 names the realizing of it. The Awakening of Faith treatise systematizes this distinction, and Tendai theology elaborates it into a rich account of the relationship between intrinsic and acquired modes of awakening.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present in Japanese writing from the Nara period as the standard term for 'beginning, inception.' Its doctrinal use in 始覚 arrives with the Heian Tendai transmission of the Awakening of Faith and its commentaries — Saichō and his successors elaborate the 本覚/始覚 distinction as a foundational theological frame. The doctrinal usage is technical and remains so; in ordinary modern Japanese 始 has no Buddhist resonance.

gloss: Root, origin, foundation — a tree (木) with a mark at its base; the original ground from which a thing extends, recruited in Buddhist usage to name what is intrinsically and originally the case.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 木 (tree) with a horizontal mark or thickening at its base — pointing to the root
graphic_evolution.notes: An indicative-character (指事) construction: the tree's root is marked rather than depicted as a separate object. The semantic logic is concrete and immediate — what is at the base of the tree is its root, its origin, the place it grows from.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 木 (4 strokes) with a horizontal mark crossing the central vertical at its base — 5 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: The added stroke at the base remains visible in the modern form. From 'tree's root' the meaning extends to: origin, foundation, fundamental principle, the source. In Buddhist contexts 本 marks what is original, intrinsic, prior to elaboration — as in 本来 (originally), 本性 (original nature), 本心 (original mind), 本覚 (original enlightenment).

doctrinal_notes: In 本覚 (hongaku, original enlightenment), 本 carries the load of Tendai-derived doctrine that awakening is not acquired through practice but is the originally and always already realized condition of all beings. The character also pairs with 始 (acquired) in the doctrinal contrast 本覚/始覚, and stands at the head of many key Zen compounds: 本来面目 (original face), 本心 (original mind), 本性 (original nature). In each case 本 names not a temporal first but a structural prior — what is the case before delusion's elaboration ever began.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present from the earliest stratum of Japanese literacy and immediately mapped onto the native Japanese word もと (root, origin, basis). Its doctrinal weight in Japanese Buddhism develops in two waves: first with the Heian-period transmission of Tendai (Saichō, early 9th century), which makes 本覚 a central theological category; and again with Kamakura Zen, where 本来面目 (original face) and related compounds carry Chan's claim that what practice realizes is what was always already the case. The character is also one of the most common in modern Japanese — used as the counter for long thin objects, as the word for 'book' (本), and in countless compounds — but its philosophical register remains marked and Buddhist.

gloss: Wakefulness, awareness — to wake from sleep, to come to perception; in Buddhist usage the standing condition of being awake, contrasted with the event-character of 悟.

graphic_evolution.form_description: An upper element related to 學 (learn) over 見 (see) — perception arising through learning, or seeing in the wakeful state
graphic_evolution.notes: Traditional 覺 is a complex character; the simplified 覚 (used in modern Japanese and simplified Chinese) preserves the 見 base. The semantic core: a wakeful mode of perception, contrasted with the dulled or sleeping mode.

graphic_evolution.form_description: An upper canopy element over 見 (see, 7 strokes) — 12 strokes total in the Japanese simplified form
graphic_evolution.notes: The simplified form preserves 見 at the bottom — wakefulness as a kind of seeing. In Buddhist usage 覚 covers both the act of waking up and the standing condition of being awake; it is thus a more processual and ongoing term than 悟, which names the discrete event of awakening.

doctrinal_notes: 覚 is the Sino-Japanese character most often used to render Sanskrit buddha (the awakened one) in its etymological aspect — a Buddha is one who has woken up. The compound 仏 (Buddha) originally transliterated the Sanskrit phonetically, but doctrinal commentary consistently glosses it through 覚. In Tendai and Zen, 覚 functions as a standing condition rather than an event: 本覚 (original wakefulness) and 始覚 (acquired wakefulness) are paired as the two doctrinal poles of how beings are related to their awakened nature. Dōgen often prefers 覚 over 悟 precisely because 覚 names a continuous condition rather than a one-time breakthrough — practice as the activity of an already-awake nature, not a striving toward a future event.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the first wave of Buddhist scriptural translation in the Nara period, where it is the standard character for translating buddha as 'the awakened one' and for the verbal sense of waking up. Its doctrinal sophistication develops through Heian Tendai: Saichō's introduction of the 本覚思想 (hongaku shisō, original-enlightenment thought) makes 覚 a load-bearing term in Tendai theology, and from Tendai it passes into the later Kamakura schools — including Zen, where Dōgen engages it critically. In ordinary modern Japanese the character has a wide range of uses: 覚える (oboeru, to remember/learn), 覚醒 (kakusei, awakening from sleep), 感覚 (kankaku, sensation) — the doctrinal register survives in fixed Buddhist compounds.

gloss: Gradually, by degrees — water seeping in increments; in 漸悟 the structural opposite of 頓, marking awakening conceived as progressive deepening rather than instantaneous breakthrough.

components.meaning: water radical (3 strokes) — the semantic element: a process of incremental seeping or flowing
to cut (11 strokes) — phonetic element providing the jiàn sound, originally also semantic in the sense of cutting into stages

graphic_evolution.form_description: 氵 (water) beside 斬 (phonetic) — water flowing or soaking in by stages
graphic_evolution.notes: The original sense draws on the image of water soaking gradually into ground or rising slowly in a vessel — a process whose direction is unmistakable but whose progress is incremental. From this concrete sense the abstract meaning of 'gradually, by degrees' emerges in classical usage.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 氵 (3 strokes) on the left beside 斬 (11 strokes) — 14 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 漸 survives in formal compounds: 漸進 (zenshin, gradual progress), 漸増 (zenzō, gradual increase), 漸次 (zenji, by degrees). Its load-bearing doctrinal use is 漸悟 — paired structurally with 頓悟 in the Tang-dynasty Chan controversy and inherited as a conceptual frame in Japanese Zen.

doctrinal_notes: In 漸悟 (zengo), 漸 marks awakening as something approached through stages — the Northern school's position that purification of obscurations and the deepening of insight occur progressively. The character does not name slowness so much as cumulative direction: the practitioner advances by degrees toward a result that is not given all at once. The polemical opposition with 頓 in Chan historiography is so structurally rigid that 漸 in Buddhist contexts almost never appears without its sudden counterpart in the background.
japanese_transmission: Arrives in Japan as part of the general classical-Chinese vocabulary in the Nara period, where it carries the ordinary sense of 'gradually, step by step.' Its consequential Buddhist register is Kamakura: the 頓漸 (sudden-gradual) controversy reaches Japan with the Chan transmission, and 漸悟 enters Japanese Zen discourse as the structural foil against which 頓悟 is defined. Outside of doctrinal contexts, 漸 in modern Japanese is restricted to formal and literary compounds — it has no everyday colloquial life.

gloss: Sudden, abrupt, all-at-once — a head bowing abruptly to the ground; in 頓悟 the Southern school's defining qualifier, marking awakening as instantaneous rather than cumulative.

components.meaning: to assemble, to gather — phonetic element providing the dùn sound, 4 strokes
head, page — the semantic element: a head bowing or stopping abruptly, 9 strokes

graphic_evolution.form_description: 頁 (head) beside 屯 (phonetic) — the head component signals the abrupt bowing or stopping motion that gives 頓 its core meaning
graphic_evolution.notes: The primary classical meaning: to bow the head to the ground in salutation or submission, especially the abrupt full-body prostration of kowtow. Extended to: sudden, abrupt, all-at-once. The motion of bowing — the entire body's sudden shift from upright to prostrate — provides the image of instantaneous transformation.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 屯 (4 strokes) on the left beside 頁 (head/page radical, 9 strokes) — 13 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: 頁 as a radical appears in characters involving the head: 頭 (head), 顔 (face), 願 (to wish/vow — bowing the head in prayer). 頓 among them names the abrupt movement of the head — and by extension any abrupt, all-at-once quality. The contrast with 漸 (gradual, step-by-step) is structurally built in: 頓悟 vs 漸悟 is the defining fault-line of Tang-dynasty Chan.

doctrinal_notes: In 頓悟 (tongo), 頓 marks the Southern school's doctrinal claim that genuine awakening arrives instantaneously — not at the end of a cumulative path of purification but as a single breakthrough that is complete in itself. The opposing term is 漸悟 (zengo, gradual awakening), associated with the Northern school's teaching of progressive purification. The Platform Sutra presents Huineng's sudden-awakening teaching as the authentic Chan transmission precisely against the gradual approach: 頓 is not mere quickness but a claim about the structure of awakening itself — it does not arrive in stages because there is no gap between recognizing one's nature and being it.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the Chan transmission in the Kamakura period (late 12th–13th centuries), embedded in the vocabulary of the Platform Sutra and the Rinzai koan curriculum. The 頓漸 (tonzen, sudden-gradual) debate was a live doctrinal controversy in Tang-dynasty China and remains a conceptual framework in Japanese Zen: Rinzai tends to foreground 頓 (sudden breakthrough through koan), while Sōtō tends to sidestep the contrast through shikantaza (just sitting), which Dōgen frames as practice-realization rather than either sudden or gradual. In modern Japanese, 頓 survives primarily in set compounds: 頓悟 (sudden awakening), 頓死 (sudden death), 頓智 (quick wit, quick-wittedness) — all carrying the sense of an abrupt, complete event.

gloss: Battle, combat, contest — a weapon wielded in ordered struggle; in 法戦 the character transfers the figure of combat from the battlefield to the ritual exchange of dharma questions.

components.meaning: single, simple (9 strokes in simplified form) — phonetic element providing the sen sound
dagger-axe, weapon (4 strokes) — the semantic radical: a tool of war, placing the character in the sphere of armed struggle

graphic_evolution.form_description: An element involving 戈 (dagger-axe) alongside a phonetic component — the weapon marks the semantic field of combat
graphic_evolution.notes: The traditional form 戰 is more complex; the modern Japanese 戦 is a simplification standardized in the postwar shinjitai reform. The semantic core is stable across all periods: 戈 signals that whatever is happening, it is happening as armed contest — a meeting of forces under the sign of a weapon.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 単 (9 strokes) on the left beside 戈 (4 strokes) — 13 strokes total in the Japanese simplified form
graphic_evolution.notes: In ordinary Japanese 戦 covers war (戦争), battle (戦闘), strategy (戦略), and the verbal sense of fighting or trembling (戦く). Its figural use in 法戦 preserves the combat register while transferring the action onto the field of dharma exchange — the sense is not metaphorical softening but tonal intensification.

doctrinal_notes: In 法戦式 (hossenshiki, dharma combat ceremony), 戦 is doing figural work that readers sometimes want to defuse but should not. The compound names the formal ritual exchange of Zen questions and answers as a literal combat — not a softened or metaphorical one. The figure preserves what ordinary pedagogical language flattens: that the encounter between practitioners is an occasion for the strongest forces each can bring to bear on the dharma, and that the ritual structure is the arena in which this contest is held. Related compounds: 法戦 (dharma combat), 戦闘 (combat, battle).
japanese_transmission: First-wave character present in Japanese writing from the earliest periods as the standard rendering for war, battle, and contest. The traditional form 戰 was simplified to 戦 in the postwar shinjitai reform. The figural Buddhist register of 法戦 arrives with the Chan transmission in the Kamakura period, embedded in the monastic vocabulary of Rinzai and Sōtō Zen. The everyday range and the specialized monastic use coexist without confusion because the compound 法戦 signals the religious context unambiguously.

gloss: Form, pattern, ceremony — a prescribed shape that an action takes; in monastic vocabulary the ritual container that distinguishes formal from informal observance.

components.meaning: work, craft (3 strokes) — semantic element: a structured, made form
stake, peg (3 strokes) — phonetic element providing the shì sound

graphic_evolution.form_description: 工 (work) beside 弋 (phonetic stake) — a crafted form fixed to a standard
graphic_evolution.notes: The semantic core is stable from early usage: a prescribed form or pattern that an action or object conforms to. From this concrete sense the range extends to: ritual ceremony (the form that an observance takes), mathematical formula (the standard shape of an equation), model or type.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 工 (3 strokes) beside 弋 (3 strokes) — 6 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 式 is among the most productive suffixes for naming ceremonies: 結婚式 (wedding), 卒業式 (graduation), 入学式 (enrollment). In monastic contexts it marks the distinction between the ritually bounded event and the ongoing flow of practice: 法戦式 is a ceremony in the strong sense, with defined beginning, defined form, and defined end.

doctrinal_notes: In 法戦式 (hossenshiki), 式 marks the exchange as a ceremony — a ritually bounded event with prescribed form — rather than a spontaneous occurrence of dharma combat. The distinction matters: 法戦 alone could name any vigorous encounter between practitioners; 法戦式 names the specific ritual event that marks a practice period's culmination, with choreographed questions, responses, and public witnessing. The suffix 式 places the combat within a ceremonial container that distinguishes it from the informal exchanges of daily monastic life.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character present in Japanese from the Nara period as the standard term for a prescribed form or pattern. Its productivity as a ceremonial suffix develops through the classical period and remains dominant in modern Japanese: nearly every named ceremony in contemporary Japanese life is constructed as X式. The monastic use in 法戦式 arrives with the Chan transmission in the Kamakura period and is preserved in both Rinzai and Sōtō training regulations.

gloss: Room, chamber — an enclosed interior space under a roof; in 室内 the particular inner chamber where a master receives students for private, esoteric instruction.

components.meaning: roof radical (3 strokes) — the semantic frame: an enclosed space under a roof
to arrive, to reach (6 strokes) — phonetic and partially semantic element: the place one arrives at, the destination within a dwelling

graphic_evolution.form_description: 宀 (roof) over 至 (arriving) — a place one enters beneath a roof
graphic_evolution.notes: The original sense is concrete: an inner room of a dwelling, the chamber one reaches after entering the outer hall. From this concrete sense the meaning extends to any enclosed or bounded interior space: a room, a chamber, a compartment.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 宀 (3 strokes) over 至 (6 strokes) — 9 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In ordinary Japanese 室 names a room, a cabin, or a bounded interior: 教室 (classroom), 居室 (living room), 研究室 (laboratory). In Zen monastic use 室 is the master's private chamber — the room where dokusan is given and where the transmitted, esoteric core of the lineage is held. The compound 室内 (within the room) carries this specialized sense.

doctrinal_notes: In 室内 (shitsunai, within the room), 室 names the physical and institutional space of the master's private chamber where one-on-one interviews occur and where the lineage's transmitted teachings — its koan curriculum, its encounter language, its esoteric answers — are passed directly from teacher to student. The figure is architectural and exclusive: 室内 marks what is inside the room as doctrinally distinct from what is said in public teishō or jōdō. The term's technical use in Rinzai curriculum contexts (where 'the shitsunai' names the entire body of transmitted koan responses held within a lineage) preserves this spatial logic.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character present in Japanese from the Nara period as the standard term for a room or inner chamber. Its monastic specialization arrives with the Chan transmission in the Kamakura period, embedded in the vocabulary of the dokusan interview and the transmitted koan curriculum. In modern Japanese the ordinary architectural sense and the specialized monastic sense coexist: 室内 in everyday Japanese means simply 'indoors,' while in Zen contexts it names the specific interior of the master's teaching chamber.

gloss: Inside, within, inner — what lies enclosed within a boundary; in 室内 the spatial preposition that locates teaching within the master's private chamber rather than the public hall.

components.meaning: open box radical (2 strokes) — the semantic frame: a bounded enclosure
to enter (2 strokes) — semantic element: the act of going inside the enclosure

graphic_evolution.form_description: An enclosure with an element showing entry — the figure of something going inside a bounded space
graphic_evolution.notes: The original image is concrete: something or someone entering into an enclosed space. From this concrete sense the abstract meaning of 'interior, inside, within' develops in classical usage, along with the relational sense of 'inner' as opposed to 'outer' (内外, nèiwài, inside and outside).

graphic_evolution.form_description: 冂 (2 strokes) enclosing 入 (2 strokes) — 4 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 内 is one of the most common characters and carries a wide range of relational senses: inside (内部), within (以内), one's own household (家内), and the socially intimate in-group (the distinction 内/外 structures much of Japanese social vocabulary). The monastic use in 室内 is a specialization of the general spatial sense.

doctrinal_notes: In 室内 (shitsunai, within the room), 内 does the relational work of distinguishing what is held inside the master's chamber from what is communicated outside it. The character is not neutral: it carries the social and doctrinal weight of the Japanese 内/外 distinction, marking what is in-group and esoteric as opposed to what is public and exoteric. In the Rinzai koan curriculum, 内 is the spatial figure for the transmitted, lineage-internal answers that have no public circulation — what is 室内 stays 室内.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character present in Japanese writing from the earliest periods and mapped directly onto the native Japanese word うち (uchi, inside, within, one's own group). The character's productivity in modern Japanese is enormous, covering physical interiority, social in-group membership, temporal boundaries (within a period), and possessive intimacy. The monastic specialization in 室内 borrows the general spatial and relational sense without modification.

gloss: Public, open, official — what belongs to or is visible to the common body rather than held in private; in 公案 the character that marks a Chan case as publicly circulating precedent rather than private instruction.

components.meaning: to divide, to part (2 strokes) — semantic element: opening out, making accessible
private, enclosed (2 strokes) — the semantic element that 八 opens: the private broken open into the public

graphic_evolution.form_description: 八 (divide) over 厶 (private) — the classical etymology reads this as the opening up of what was private into the public sphere
graphic_evolution.notes: The Shuowen Jiezi glosses 公 as the opposite of 私 (private) — what is 公 is what is opened out from private enclosure into common access. From this core sense come the meanings of public office (公務), official (公式), and common/shared (公共). The figure is ethical as well as spatial: 公 names fairness and impartiality, the stance that does not enclose what should be shared.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 八 (2 strokes) over 厶 (2 strokes) — 4 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 公 is productive across many registers: 公共 (public), 公的 (official), 公園 (public park), 公平 (fairness). The compound 公案 (kōan) preserves an older Tang-dynasty legal sense — 公 as 'official, pertaining to the magistrate's court' — where 公案 originally named a public legal case before being recruited by Chan as a figure for the recorded precedents of master-student encounter.

doctrinal_notes: In 公案 (kōan), 公 is not doing the general 'public' work the character carries in modern Japanese but a more specific legal-administrative work. The compound originates in Tang-dynasty magistrate's vocabulary: 公案 is the magistrate's public docket, the case-file held on the official's desk that records a precedent-setting judgment. Chan teachers recruited the term to name the recorded encounters of the ancestors precisely because the legal figure preserved the sense of precedent — a case decided, publicly attested, and available as a standard against which later cases can be measured. The ethical and juridical resonances are inseparable from the pedagogical use.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character present in Japanese writing from the Nara period and mapped onto the native Japanese word おおやけ (ōyake, public, open, the common). Its productivity in ordinary Japanese covers public office, common access, and impartial fairness. The specialized Chan use in 公案 arrives in Japan with the Kamakura Zen transmission and preserves the Tang-dynasty legal register that most contemporary Japanese speakers no longer hear when they use the character in everyday contexts.

gloss: Case, file, docket, plan — a wooden writing-table on which documents are laid out for consideration; in 公案 the record of a Chan encounter treated as a precedent-setting case.

components.meaning: peace, calm (6 strokes) — phonetic element providing the àn sound; also semantic in the sense of a settled place
tree, wood (4 strokes) — the semantic radical: a wooden object, originally a writing-table or desk

graphic_evolution.form_description: 安 (settled) over 木 (wood) — a wooden surface on which things are laid to be settled
graphic_evolution.notes: The original concrete meaning is a low wooden writing-table or tray — the surface on which a magistrate laid out documents for consideration. From this concrete sense the meaning extends metonymically: first to the documents laid on the table (a case, a file), then to the content of those documents (a plan, a proposal, a judgment). The compound 公案 preserves the earliest juridical sense: the magistrate's official case-file.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 安 (6 strokes) over 木 (4 strokes) — 10 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 案 is productive in the extended senses: 案内 (guidance, information), 提案 (proposal), 法案 (legislative bill), 案件 (case, matter). The concrete sense of the wooden table is no longer active, but the metonymy from table to case-file to proposal is preserved in the compound vocabulary. The Chan use in 公案 holds closest to the early juridical sense: the case before the bench.

doctrinal_notes: In 公案 (kōan), 案 carries the juridical and documentary sense that has faded from most modern uses of the character. The compound 公案 is literally 'public case-file' — the magistrate's docket on which precedent-setting judgments are recorded. Chan teachers recruited the figure because the magistrate's case-file is not a discussion or a hypothesis but a recorded decision: the case was decided, the precedent is set, and later cases can be measured against it. The Zen koan inherits this structure — the ancestor's response to the question was not an opinion to be weighed but a move that decided the matter, and the student approaches it as a living precedent to meet rather than a puzzle to solve.
japanese_transmission: Arrives in Japanese in the Nara period as part of the general classical-Chinese vocabulary, initially with its range of administrative and documentary senses. Its specialized Chan use in 公案 enters Japan with the Kamakura Zen transmission, where the legal-documentary figure is preserved as a technical term of Rinzai koan practice. In modern Japanese the ordinary senses (proposal, plan, case) have taken over everyday use, and the specialized koan sense is restricted to Zen contexts.

gloss: Speech, talk, story — what is said in words; in 話頭 the character that names the spoken utterance whose 'head' (critical phrase) becomes the object of meditative concentration.

components.meaning: speech, word (7 strokes) — the semantic radical: the register of spoken language
tongue (6 strokes) — phonetic and semantic element: the organ of speech, the physical source of the spoken word

graphic_evolution.form_description: 言 (speech) beside 舌 (tongue) — speech coming from the tongue
graphic_evolution.notes: The semantic logic is transparent: speech produced by the physical organ of speech. From this concrete sense the meaning extends to: what is said, a conversation, a story, a topic of discussion. The character is structurally and semantically linked to 言 (word) but names specifically the embodied, vocal aspect of speech rather than the abstract word.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 言 (7 strokes) beside 舌 (6 strokes) — 13 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 話 is among the most common characters and covers speech in nearly all its registers: 会話 (conversation), 電話 (telephone), 童話 (children's story), 話題 (topic). The Chan-specific use in 話頭 preserves the sense of 'a particular utterance' — the specific saying or exchange that the practitioner concentrates on — rather than speech in general.

doctrinal_notes: In 話頭 (watō, Ch. huàtóu, 'head of the word'), 話 names the complete utterance — the koan as a whole saying or exchange — from which the 頭 (head, critical phrase) is isolated for meditative concentration. The distinction between 話 and 話頭 is structural and important: 話 is the whole story or dialogue, 話頭 is the single critical phrase or word at its decisive point that the practitioner holds in awareness. In Dahui Zonggao's kanhua (koan-introspecting) method, the practitioner does not meditate on the full 話 but on its 頭 — the pivotal point where the meaning concentrates.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character present in Japanese writing from the Nara period as the standard rendering for speech and spoken discourse. Its everyday productivity in modern Japanese is enormous — 話 is among the first characters Japanese children learn. The specialized Chan use in 話頭 arrives with the Kamakura transmission and remains a technical term of Rinzai kōan practice, preserving the Dahui-derived distinction between the full utterance and its critical phrase.

gloss: Head, top, beginning — the head of a body, by extension the top or leading edge of anything; in 話頭 the decisive critical phrase at the head of a koan utterance.

components.meaning: bean, ritual vessel (7 strokes) — phonetic element providing the tóu sound
head, page radical (9 strokes) — the semantic element: a head, the top of the body

graphic_evolution.form_description: 豆 (phonetic) beside 頁 (head radical) — a head, with phonetic marker
graphic_evolution.notes: The semantic core is stable: the head of a body. From this concrete sense the meaning extends to the top, the leading edge, the beginning, or the principal point of anything — a counting suffix for animals (頭 for cattle and horses), the leader of a group (頭目), the start of something (頭から). The sense of 'the critical or decisive point' emerges naturally from 'the head of the matter.'

graphic_evolution.form_description: 豆 (7 strokes) beside 頁 (9 strokes) — 16 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 頭 is one of the most semantically rich characters: anatomical head (頭), the top of something (先頭), a numerical counter for large animals, leader (頭領). The Chan specialization in 話頭 draws on the sense of 'the head, the point, the critical place' — not the anatomical head but the decisive locus of a matter.

doctrinal_notes: In 話頭 (watō, huàtóu), 頭 does not mean 'head' in the anatomical sense but 'the head of' — the leading edge, the critical point, the place where the matter concentrates. The compound names the single phrase or word at which the koan's entire force converges: in Zhaozhou's 'Does a dog have Buddha-nature? — 無,' the 話頭 is the bare 無, the point at which the whole exchange concentrates. Dahui Zonggao's kanhua method instructs the practitioner to hold this critical phrase in awareness without interpretation, letting it function as the focal point of meditative inquiry. The choice of 頭 for this function is precise: not the whole body of the saying but the head, not the full argument but its decisive edge.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character present in Japanese writing from the Nara period and mapped onto the native Japanese words あたま (atama, head), かしら (kashira, head, chief), and こうべ (kōbe, head, literary). Its productivity across registers — anatomical, positional, and figurative — is characteristic of the oldest stratum of Sino-Japanese vocabulary. The specialized Chan use in 話頭 arrives with the Kamakura transmission and preserves the figurative sense of 'the leading edge of a matter' that was already active in Tang-dynasty colloquial Chinese when Dahui systematized the kanhua method.

gloss: To watch, to look at with sustained attention — a hand shading the eye to see far; in Chan 看話 the word for the practitioner's concentrated inward looking at the critical phrase of a koan.

components.meaning: hand (4 strokes, written 龵 when stacked above 目) — the semantic element raised as a visor above the eye
eye (5 strokes) — the semantic element: the organ of sight, shielded by the hand to see into the distance

graphic_evolution.form_description: A hand placed above an eye — the pictographic figure of shading the eye to see far
graphic_evolution.notes: The semantic logic is transparent and unusually vivid: a hand raised above the eye as a visor, the bodily posture of someone straining to see into the distance. From this concrete sense the meaning extends to concentrated looking in general, and then to watching over, minding, guarding. The character preserves its pictorial clarity into the modern form more than most compounds of this era.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 手 above 目 — 9 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 看 is less common as a standalone word than 見 but is productive in compounds of sustained attention: 看病 (nursing the sick), 看守 (guard), 看板 (signboard, literally 'board to be looked at'), 看過 (overlook). The sense of 'looking at with concentration and duration' — rather than a single glance — is what survives across all these uses.

doctrinal_notes: In 看話禅 (kanna zen, Ch. kànhuà chán) the character names the practitioner's mode of looking at the 話頭 — not an analytical reading, not a glancing notice, but the sustained, concentrated, non-discursive regard of the critical phrase as a focal point of meditative inquiry. The figure of the hand shading the eye to see far is apt: the practitioner is not looking at what is near and obvious but at something that requires effort of attention to see at all. Dahui Zonggao's choice of 看 (rather than, say, 念 to 'keep in mind' or 思 to 'think about') is doctrinally precise — the 話頭 is held as something seen, not as something thought. Looking, unlike thinking, can be sustained without generating content.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the general Sino-Japanese lexical stock in the early literary period and becomes technical vocabulary in Rinzai Zen through the transmission of kànhuà (kanna) practice from Song-dynasty China. In the modern Japanese lexicon 看 is firmly settled in the register of sustained attention and care — the connotations that 看病 and 看護 (nursing) preserve — which aligns with the Chan use of 看 for the practitioner's concentrated looking at a koan phrase.

gloss: Word, speech, saying — the articulate unit of discourse; in Chan 着語 / 下語 the short phrase offered as a compressed response to a koan case.

components.meaning: speech, word (7 strokes) — the semantic radical: the register of articulate language
I, myself (7 strokes) — phonetic element providing the yǔ / go sound; the pairing suggests speech as what issues from a speaker

graphic_evolution.form_description: 言 (speech) beside 吾 (I) — speech as what comes from the self
graphic_evolution.notes: The pairing is phonetic in origin (吾 supplies the yǔ / go sound) but semantically suggestive: speech as what issues from a particular speaker, not the abstract phenomenon of language but the spoken word of someone. The character is one of the densely productive speech-family graphs built on 言.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 言 (7 strokes) beside 吾 (7 strokes) — 14 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 語 covers both the concrete sense of a spoken word or phrase and the abstract sense of language as a system: 単語 (vocabulary word), 物語 (tale, narrative), 日本語 (the Japanese language), 語学 (language study).
