In the Chan technical senses 着語 and 下語 the character carries the concrete sense — a specific phrase produced as a response — rather than the abstract.

doctrinal_notes: In 着語 (jakugo, capping phrase) and 下語 (agyo, appended words), 語 names the short phrase offered as a compressed response to a koan case. The grammatical figure is important: the 語 is a particular thing said, not the general category of speech; it is offered, placed, or set down in response to a specific case. The Rinzai koan curriculum treats these phrases as units of demonstration — a phrase that fits the case is an enactment of insight, a phrase that does not fit is rejected — and the phrase books (notably the Zenrin Kushū) catalog the repertoire from which students draw. 語 here is not speech as expression of opinion but speech as matched offering.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character among the productive core of the Sino-Japanese speech-family graphs, present in Japanese writing from the Nara period. Its productivity in compounds is enormous and the character is internalized at the most basic stratum of Japanese literacy. The Chan specializations 着語 and 下語 arrive with the Kamakura transmission of koan practice and remain technical vocabulary of Rinzai monastic training.

gloss: Silence, to be silent — the suspension of speech; in Chan 黙照 the silence paired with illumination as the two inseparable aspects of zazen in the Caodong/Sōtō tradition.

components.meaning: black (11 strokes) — semantic and phonetic element: the dark, the obscured, the register of what is withdrawn from view or voice
dog (4 strokes) — semantic element; the traditional etymology invokes a dog going after prey silently, without barking

graphic_evolution.form_description: 黒 (black) beside 犬 (dog) — the standard etymological account invokes the figure of a dog stalking silently
graphic_evolution.notes: The Shuowen Jiezi explains the character as 'a dog that follows silently in pursuit,' and whatever the actual origin, the association of the character with the suspension of vocal expression is stable across its history. The 黒 component also contributes the phonetic value mò / moku and suggests the register of the darkened, the unspoken, the withdrawn from view.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 黒 (11 strokes) with 犬 (4 strokes) beside it — 15 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 黙 is active in compounds of silence and the refusal of speech: 沈黙 (silence), 黙想 (silent contemplation), 黙殺 (to ignore without comment), 暗黙 (tacit). The character is at home in the register of the deliberately unvoiced — not mere absence of sound but the chosen holding-back of speech.

doctrinal_notes: In 黙照禅 (mokushō zen, Ch. mòzhào chán), 黙 names the silence that the Caodong/Sōtō tradition pairs with 照 (illumination) as the two inseparable aspects of zazen. The silence is not mental blankness but the suspension of discursive proliferation — the stilling of the mind's habit of producing internal commentary — and Hongzhi Zhengjue's Silent Illumination Inscription (黙照銘) holds that this silence is not preparatory to illumination but is already one face of it. Dahui Zonggao's polemic against 黙照 charged that silence without an object collapses into torpor, and the Linji/Rinzai–Caodong/Sōtō methodological difference turns on whether 黙 is adequate as a meditative condition or requires the focal point of a 話頭 to function.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the general Sino-Japanese lexical stock and is present in Japanese literary language from the earliest Buddhist texts. Its compounds for silence and the unvoiced — 沈黙, 暗黙, 黙想 — are fully naturalized in modern Japanese across all registers. The Chan technical use in 黙照禅 arrives with the Kamakura transmission of Caodong/Sōtō practice through Dōgen and preserves the specific doctrinal sense of silence as one of two inseparable aspects of zazen rather than as mere quietness.

gloss: To illuminate, to shine upon, to clarify — the steady lighting of what is there to be seen; in Chan 黙照 the clear awareness paired with silence as the two faces of zazen.

components.meaning: bright, clear (9 strokes) — phonetic and partially semantic element: the clarity that the lower fire-radical then specifies as shining or illuminating
fire radical (variant of 火, 4 strokes) — the semantic element: the source of light that does the illuminating

graphic_evolution.form_description: 昭 (bright) above 灬 (fire) — brightness with fire as its source
graphic_evolution.notes: The layered construction is semantically and phonetically ordered: 昭 is both 'bright' and the phonetic marker providing the zhào sound; the fire radical below specifies that this brightness is the brightness of a light source actively shining, not a static state. The result names the activity of illumination — the ongoing casting of light upon something.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 昭 (9 strokes) above 灬 (4 strokes) — 13 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 照 is productive across literal and figurative registers: 照明 (lighting), 照合 (cross-checking, literally 'illuminating to match'), 照会 (inquiry, literally 'illuminating an encounter'), 日照 (sunshine), 参照 (reference, literally 'illuminating alongside'). The sense of 'making clear by shining light upon' ranges from the physical to the cognitive without shifting its fundamental meaning.

doctrinal_notes: In 黙照禅 (mokushō zen), 照 names the luminous clarity of awareness that the Caodong/Sōtō tradition holds as the inseparable companion of 黙 (silence). The figure is precise and doctrinally load-bearing: 照 is not a cognitive grasping or conceptual understanding but the clear, steady lighting of what is present in the meditative field, the aspect of zazen under which phenomena are seen as they are without the interference of discursive commentary. Hongzhi Zhengjue's Silent Illumination Inscription insists that 黙 and 照 are one activity and not two stages — the silence illuminates, the illumination is silent — and this insistence is what Dōgen radicalizes as the unity-formula of shikantaza and practice-realization.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the general Sino-Japanese lexical stock in the early literary period and is productive in Japanese from the Heian period onward across literal and figurative senses of illumination and clarification. The Chan technical use in 黙照 arrives with the Kamakura transmission of Caodong/Sōtō practice and is preserved in Dōgen's writings, where the Chinese inscription's pairing of silence and illumination is inherited and radicalized rather than merely translated.

gloss: To attach, to apply, to come to rest upon — the act of fastening one thing onto another; in Chan 着語 the figure of appending a capping phrase to a recorded koan case.

components.meaning: undivided — a variant simplification of 著, itself composed of the grass radical 艹 over 者 (person); the modern 着 form conventionalizes the lower portion and does not decompose cleanly in contemporary use

graphic_evolution.form_description: A variant of 著, historically interchangeable with it
graphic_evolution.notes: 着 and 著 were historically the same character and diverged in their orthographic and semantic ranges over time. 著 retains the senses of 'to write, to manifest, the written, the author'; 着 specialized toward the senses of 'to attach, to wear, to come to rest upon, to touch.' In modern Japanese the two are treated as separate characters with partly overlapping readings, and the Rinzai term 着語 uses 着 with its specialized sense of attachment.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 12 strokes; conventionalized simplified form of the classical 著 variant
graphic_evolution.notes: 着 is firmly settled in the register of physical and figurative attachment: 着陸 (landing, literally 'attaching to the land'), 到着 (arrival, 'reaching and attaching'), 着用 (to put on, to wear), 執着 (clinging, attachment — the Buddhist sense of unwholesome fixation). The character carries the image of one thing coming to rest upon another and not departing, a figure that the Rinzai term 着語 exploits precisely.

doctrinal_notes: In 着語 (jakugo, capping phrase), 着 names the act of fastening a short phrase to a recorded koan case — not a commentary that stands apart from the case but a phrase that comes to rest upon it, that attaches to it as a compressed matching response. The figure is spatial and precise: the 着語 adheres to the case rather than explaining it at a distance. The doctrinal stake is that the capping phrase enacts insight by fitting the case, and the 着 (attaching) names exactly this fit. A phrase that does not adhere is simply a phrase; a phrase that does is a 着語. The pronunciation じゃく in this compound — rather than the more common ちゃく — preserves an older Sino-Japanese reading active in Chan technical vocabulary.
japanese_transmission: Arrives in Japanese writing as a classical variant of 著 and settles into its modern specialized range during the medieval period. Its productivity in compounds of attachment, arrival, and wearing is characteristic of the practical Sino-Japanese vocabulary. The Chan technical use in 着語 is a specialized survival of the older Buddhist sense of 'attachment' — in its neutral rather than afflictive register, naming the good kind of fit between phrase and case rather than the unwholesome clinging of 執着.

gloss: Below, beneath, to put down — the spatial region beneath a reference line; in Chan 下語 the figure of setting words down beside a koan case as the practitioner's offered response.

components.meaning: undivided — oracle-bone and bronze forms depict a short mark below a long horizontal line, the spatial indicator of 'beneath'; a pictographic indicator sign rather than a decomposable compound

graphic_evolution.form_description: A short stroke beneath a long horizontal line — an indicator sign for 'below'
graphic_evolution.notes: 下 and its counterpart 上 are among the earliest Chinese characters of the indicator-sign type — neither pictographs nor compounds but abstract spatial markers. The long horizontal is the reference line; the short stroke below names the region beneath it. The character is thus semantically pure: it names a spatial relationship directly, without metaphor or phonetic borrowing.

graphic_evolution.form_description: Horizontal line above, a short vertical with a small stroke below — 3 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 下 covers the entire range of 'beneath' and 'downward' across literal, figurative, and honorific registers: 下る (to descend, to be given), 下さる (honorific 'to give'), 下位 (lower rank), 地下 (underground), 閣下 (His Excellency, literally 'beneath the chamber'). Its productivity is enormous and its semantic range unusually wide for a 3-stroke character.

doctrinal_notes: In 下語 (agyo, appended words), 下 names the act of setting or putting words down — not speaking them in debate but placing them as an offering beside the koan case. The figure is important: the student does not argue about the case, does not construct an interpretation to be defended, but sets words down and waits to see whether they fit. The verb 下す (kudasu, to put down, to hand down) is doing the semantic work here, and the parallel with 着 in 着語 is instructive. 着 names the attaching, 下 names the placing; both name modes of offering a phrase to a case without claiming to explain it. The Rinzai tradition's use of 下 in this technical sense preserves the spatial figure of the student's phrase as a thing placed, not a thing argued.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character among the earliest Sino-Japanese vocabulary, present in Japanese writing from the Nara period and internalized at the most basic stratum of Japanese literacy. Its enormous productivity across literal, figurative, and honorific registers — from 下さる (honorific 'to give') to 地下 (underground) to 下剋上 (low overthrowing high) — makes it one of the most semantically loaded short characters in the lexicon. The specialized Chan use in 下語 is a technical survival that draws on the everyday sense of 'putting down' and redirects it to the ritual setting of koan training.

gloss: To pick up between the fingers, to take hold of delicately — a hand singling out one thing from among many; in Chan commentary the act of selecting a case or point and holding it up for the assembly's attention.

components.meaning: hand radical (simplified form of 手, 3 strokes) — the semantic element: an act of the hand
to divine, to occupy (5 strokes) — phonetic element, provides the niān/nen sound

graphic_evolution.form_description: 手 (hand) beside 占 (phonetic) — a phono-semantic compound: the hand picking something up
graphic_evolution.notes: The primary meaning is to pick up between thumb and fingers — a precise, selective grasp, not a full-handed grip. The character implies delicacy and discrimination: choosing one object from among several and lifting it for inspection.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 扌 (hand radical, 3 strokes) on the left beside 占 (5 strokes) — 8 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In Chan/Zen usage 拈 is almost exclusively institutional: 拈提 (picking up and presenting), 拈古 (picking up the old), 拈華微笑 (picking up the flower and smiling — the origin legend of the Chan transmission). The ordinary Chinese sense of 'to pinch, to twist between the fingers' recedes entirely behind the technical Buddhist usage.

doctrinal_notes: 拈 is the signature verb of Chan/Zen commentary. The foundational myth of the Chan transmission — Śākyamuni holding up a flower (拈華), Mahākāśyapa smiling — places 拈 at the origin of the lineage itself. In the koan commentary tradition 拈 names the master's act of selecting a case from the archive of recorded dialogues and lifting it into view for the current assembly, and the two compounds 拈提 and 拈古 name the two principal modes of this activity: 拈提 (picking up and presenting the essential point) and 拈古 (picking up the old case and adding commentary). The verb carries the full weight of the Chan claim that old cases are not dead archives but living instruments that must be picked up and handled anew in each generation.
japanese_transmission: Arrives with the Kamakura transmission of Chan/Zen as part of the specialized koan-commentary vocabulary. The character has no significant pre-Buddhist Japanese usage. In modern Japanese 拈 is rare outside Zen contexts — it appears in 拈華微笑 (nengemishō, the flower-holding origin story), 拈提 (nentei), and 拈古 (nenko), but not in ordinary discourse. The kun reading ひねる (to twist, to pinch) exists but is more commonly written with other characters.

gloss: Old, ancient — that which has passed through ten (十) mouths (口); in Chan usage the reservoir of recorded cases from which every generation of teachers draws its material.

components.meaning: ten (2 strokes) — the upper element: a large number, suggesting many generations
mouth (3 strokes) — the lower element: oral transmission, speech, what has been said

graphic_evolution.form_description: 十 (many, ten) above 口 (mouth) — what has passed through many mouths, what has been said for generations
graphic_evolution.notes: The traditional etymological reading: something told and retold across ten generations is old. The figure is oral rather than visual — antiquity measured not by the look of age but by the depth of its circulation through speech.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 十 (2 strokes) above 口 (3 strokes) — 5 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 古 is ubiquitous: 古い (furui, old), 古典 (koten, classics), 古代 (kodai, ancient era), 中古 (chūko, medieval or secondhand). In Chan/Zen technical usage it appears in 拈古 (picking up the old), 頌古 (verse on the old), 古則 (old rule/case) — all naming activities that take the archive of ancient cases as their raw material.

doctrinal_notes: In the koan commentary vocabulary, 古 names the temporal location of the cases being worked: they are old, ancient, from the founding generations. But the Chan claim is that 古 is not merely a historical descriptor — picking up the old (拈古) and versifying the old (頌古) are live acts of engagement with cases that, despite their antiquity, are not resolved or exhausted. The old case remains open to each generation's handling, and the 古 marks not the case's closure but the depth of the tradition that has handled it before the current practitioner takes it up.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present from the earliest stratum of Japanese literacy and mapped onto the native Japanese word ふるい (old). Its Zen-technical usage in 拈古, 頌古, and 古則 arrives with the Kamakura Chan transmission, layering a specialized commentarial vocabulary onto a character that was already entirely naturalized in ordinary Japanese discourse.

gloss: Rule, standard, case — a knife (刂) cutting to the measure of a vessel (貝); in koan literature the recorded case as a decided standard against which the student's understanding is measured.

components.meaning: shell, treasure (7 strokes) — the semantic element: a valued standard, something measured and weighed
knife radical (2 strokes) — the cutting element: to cut to measure, to establish a standard

graphic_evolution.form_description: 貝 (cowrie shell / treasure) beside 刀 (knife) — to cut according to a standard, to regulate
graphic_evolution.notes: The original figure is economic and juridical: the cowrie shell as a unit of value, the knife as the instrument of measurement. From 'measuring by the standard' the meaning extends to rule, regulation, standard, and then — in koan usage — the recorded case as a precedent that sets the standard.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 貝 (7 strokes) on the left beside 刂 (knife radical, 2 strokes) — 9 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In modern Japanese 則 appears in 規則 (kisoku, regulation), 法則 (hōsoku, law/principle), 原則 (gensoku, general principle). In koan literature 本則 (honsoku, main case) and 古則 (kosoku, old case) use 則 as a counter for koan cases: one 則 is one case. The Blue Cliff Record contains 100 則.

doctrinal_notes: In 本則 (honsoku, main case), 則 names the koan case as a decided standard — a precedent recorded and available as a measure. The juridical overtone is not accidental: the Chinese term 公案 (gōng'àn, public case) is itself a legal metaphor, and 則 extends the same figure by treating the case as a unit of decided precedent that can be counted, enumerated, and presented. A koan collection is organized by 則 — the Blue Cliff Record has 100 則, the Gateless Gate 48 則 — and the student's work is to meet each 則 on the terms the precedent has set.
japanese_transmission: First-wave character, present from the Nara period in legal and administrative vocabulary. Its koan-technical usage as a counter for cases — 第一則 (the first case), 本則 (the main case) — arrives with the Kamakura-period transmission of the Song-dynasty koan collection genre. In modern Japanese 則 is common in legal and scientific registers (規則, 法則) but rare in everyday speech; its Zen usage is specialized and literary.

gloss: Verse, hymn, eulogy — a formal poetic composition recited aloud (公) before an audience (頁 as 'head,' i.e. the one who faces and recites); in koan literature the verse commentary that responds to an ancient case.

components.meaning: public, communal (4 strokes) — phonetic element, also carrying the sense of open, public recitation
head, page (9 strokes) — the semantic element: originally 'head' (the one who faces the audience), later 'page' (the surface on which text is written)

graphic_evolution.form_description: 公 (phonetic) beside 頁 (head) — a phono-semantic compound: public recitation, a verse spoken or chanted
graphic_evolution.notes: The original sense is a hymn or eulogy — a formal verse composition recited in a ceremonial or public setting. In Buddhist usage 頌 translates Sanskrit gāthā (verse), and the character carries the sense of a verse that is not merely decorative but performative: the recitation does something.

graphic_evolution.form_description: 公 (4 strokes) on the left beside 頁 (9 strokes) — 13 strokes total
graphic_evolution.notes: In Buddhist contexts 頌 appears as the standard rendering of gāthā (verse) in the sūtra translation tradition. In Chan koan commentary 頌古 (verse on an old case) names a specific genre: the poet-commentator composes a verse in response to a recorded case, and the verse is not an explanation of the case but a parallel expression of its insight in a different register. Xuedou Chongxian's hundred 頌古 on koan cases became the basis of the Blue Cliff Record.

doctrinal_notes: In 頌古 (juko/sòng gǔ, verse on an old case), 頌 names the specific literary form of the verse commentary — a poem composed in response to a koan case that demonstrates the commentator's understanding not through analysis but through the creation of a parallel aesthetic object. The genre's most celebrated practitioner is Xuedou Chongxian (980–1052), whose hundred 頌古 on koan cases were then commented on by Yuanwu Keqin to form the Blue Cliff Record. The verse commentary tradition is one of the highest literary achievements of the Chan tradition, and 頌 as a character carries the weight of that tradition: the verse that stands alongside the case as its poetic companion, not its intellectual servant.
japanese_transmission: Arrives in Japanese through two vectors: first as the standard rendering of gāthā in sūtra translation (般若心経 and other texts contain 偈頌 gejū, verse sections), and second as part of the Kamakura koan-commentary vocabulary where 頌古 names the verse-on-a-case genre. In modern Japanese 頌 is rare outside literary and Buddhist contexts — it appears in 頌歌 (shōka, ode/hymn) and in the Zen koan-commentary vocabulary, but not in everyday discourse.
